reasoning: The Federal Reserve has been running off its balance sheet, including Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS). Historically over the last year, MBS holdings have almost uniformly decreased week-over-week, shedding roughly $15-$20 billion per month. The Fed recently announced (Oct 29, 2025) that its Quantitative Tightening (QT) program will end on December 1, 2025. Crucially, the end of QT does not mean the Fed will start buying MBS again. All reports confirm the Fed intends to hold a Treasury-centric portfolio, and will allow its MBS holdings to continue rolling off (maturing/paying off). The proceeds from these maturing MBS will be reinvested into Treasury bills rather than new MBS. Therefore, MBS holdings will continue to strictly decrease throughout 2025 and 2026. The only way the question resolves YES is if there are statistical noise/settlement lags causing a transient weekly uptick relative to the base date of November 9, 2025. There are sometimes tiny week-to-week increases (e.g. +$13M in mid-October), but these are quickly wiped out by the structural $4 billion/week runoff. As the horizon grows (Nov 16, Dec 9, Feb 7), the cumulative runoff becomes massive, making a transient fluke exceeding the Nov 9 baseline virtually impossible. Probability is set very low (~2-3%) for all horizons due to the strict runoff policy, decreasing with time as the gap between current holdings and the base value widens.
